access.provider: University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative

rights.basis: The current U-M item displays a public-domain/No Copyright - United States rights designation.
rights.component: U-M digital item and electronic text
notes: This is a provider assertion for the digital item; it is distinct from the public-domain basis of the medieval texts.

entities.description: A 1993 electronic edition of twelve selected minor poems. Its header identifies the broader OTA accession U*-243-A, Smith's three-volume Poems of Robert Henryson as source, Scottish Text Society number 58 as the source volume, and the omission of printed front matter, back matter, and editorial notes.
entities.label: University of Michigan / Oxford Text Archive electronic item HenMino, U-243-A
notes: The shared OTA identifier does not establish that every U-243-A item is one encoding entity; HenMino is modeled as the identified U-M item.
No current standalone OTA accession record giving a more granular production history for this 1993 item was verified.

rights.basis: The current U-M item displays a public-domain/No Copyright - United States rights designation.
rights.component: U-M digital item and electronic text
notes: This is a provider assertion for the digital item; it is distinct from the public-domain basis of the medieval texts.

bibliographic.citation: The Poems of Robert Henryson, ed. G. Gregory Smith, vol. III, Text vol. II, Scottish Text Society 58 (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1908).
bibliographic.title: The Poems of Robert Henryson

entities.description: Scottish Text Society number 58, volume III overall and text volume II. Smith prints separately labeled witness versions, identifies the selected sources in a prefatory table, and supplies the page sequence reproduced imperfectly by HenMino's PB milestones.
entities.label: G. Gregory Smith, The Poems of Robert Henryson, volume III (1908)
notes: Smith thanks George Stevenson for transcribing and collating texts; Stevenson is therefore an edition-layer transcriber and collator, not a creator of the medieval works.

The source table counts Sum Practysis of Medecyne as 90 lines, but the printed text and XML contain a final five-line tail numbered through 91; the XML's numbered line 91 is not an extra subscription.
Smith's source table and printed headings distinguish selected versions from collateral variants.

rights.basis: Published in 1908 and publicly distributed as a full-view Internet Archive item.
rights.component: 1908 edition and scan

access.accessMethod: NLS special-collections consultation subject to the catalog's access restriction and repository approval; use the digital surrogate where sufficient
access.contact: National Library of Scotland manuscripts service

entities.description: George Bannatyne's 1568 paper anthology. The current codex has a sixty-page draft section followed by the main manuscript and was rebound into two physical volumes around 1823. HenMino's selected Smith texts use ten passages across the draft, main volume I, and main volume II.

holding.notes: The present two-volume arrangement is a nineteenth-century physical intervention; draft and main sections are textual strata within this single codex.

entities.label: Bannatyne manuscript, NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6

physicalDescription.extent: 375 folios plus sixty draft pages in the catalog description; present volume I has 192 leaves and volume II 205 leaves
physicalDescription.format: Composite poetry anthology now bound in two volumes
notes: The main manuscript has current foliation 1-370 and later pagination 1-796; that pagination is also used to locate the draft pages.

workPortion.label: Seven HenMino selected source passages in volume I
locators: Abbey Walk draft A: manuscript pp. 30-32, canvases 48-50
Prayer for the Pest main B: fols. 24r-25v, pp. 107-110, canvases 125-128

Ressoning betuix Deth and Man main B: fols. 56r-57r, pp. 171-173, canvases 189-191

Sum Practysis of Medecyne: fols. 141v-142v, pp. 342-344, canvases 360-362

notes: The catalog still displays an exhibition-unavailability notice ending in May 2025. Because that date has passed, the notice is not treated as evidence of current unavailability.

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century Scottish composite commonplace book associated with James Gray and written in several hands. It is the sole known witness for The Annunciation and supplies Smith's selected text.

holding.notes: The Annunciation is at current fols. 71r-72v; Smith and CELM use historical fols. 70r-71v.

entities.label: Gray manuscript, NLS Adv.MS.34.7.3

notes: Several hands contribute to the manuscript; association with James Gray is a compilation/provenance statement, not automatic authorship of its contents.

rights.basis: The manuscript and its text date from the late fifteenth century.
rights.component: Underlying manuscript text and material witness
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom historical-work analysis

access.accessMethod: Contact NLS rare-books staff; consult the public First Scottish Books facsimile first
access.contact: National Library of Scotland rare books service
access.entityLabel: Chepman and Myllar's 1508 print containing The Prais of Aige

bibliographic.citation: The Prais of Aige, in the Chepman and Myllar prints (Edinburgh: Walter Chepman and Androw Myllar, 1508), tract 7.
bibliographic.title: The Prais of Aige

entities.description: The anonymous thirty-two-line print version selected by Smith, published in Edinburgh in the unique NLS collection of Chepman and Myllar prints. The schema has no dedicated historical-print-witness type, so this source print is represented as a scholarly-edition entity with that limitation made explicit.

holding.notes: The current precise shelfmark was not established from the reviewed public project pages; the unique surviving collection was rebound and its leaves separately mounted in the twentieth century.

entities.label: Chepman and Myllar's 1508 print containing The Prais of Aige

rights.basis: The print was published in 1508.
rights.component: 1508 printed text and historical object
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom historical-work analysis

access.accessMethod: Advance research enquiry to the Pepys Library; general public visits are suspended during refurbishment until 2027
access.entityLabel: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553
notes: No complete public digital facsimile of Pepys MS 2553 was verified.

entities.description: A c. 1570-1585 Scots poetry anthology associated with Sir Richard Maitland. Smith prints or collates collateral versions of The Abbey Walk, Aganis Haisty Credence of Titlaris, The Ressoning betuix Aige and Zowth, and The Thre Deid Pollis; none is HenMino's selected base version.

holding.notes: The complete manuscript is not in the current online digital selection; research access is by advance contact, and the Pepys Library is closed to general visitors during refurbishment until 2027.

entities.label: Maitland Folio, Magdalene College Pepys MS 2553

notes: Several hands copied the collection.

rights.basis: The manuscript and copied texts date from the sixteenth century.
rights.component: Underlying sixteenth-century manuscript texts
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom historical-work analysis

access.contact: University of Edinburgh Centre for Research Collections
access.entityLabel: Makculloch manuscript, University of Edinburgh MS 205
access.format: Physical manuscript; selected catalog images only
notes: No complete public digital facsimile was verified, so no image bundle was cached.
access.provider: University of Edinburgh Library, Centre for Research Collections

entities.description: A composite manuscript whose 1477 Leuven logic notes belong to an earlier host layer; early-sixteenth-century Scots verse was added later on flyleaves and blanks. Smith prints or collates collateral versions of The Prais of Aige at fol. 87r and The Ressoning betuix Aige and Zowth at fol. 181v.

holding.notes: Open to consultation in the Centre for Research Collections; the public record exposes only selected images, not a complete digital facsimile.

entities.label: Makculloch manuscript, University of Edinburgh MS 205

rights.basis: The host manuscript dates to 1477 and the relevant Scots additions to the early sixteenth century.
rights.component: Underlying manuscript texts
rights.jurisdiction: United States and United Kingdom historical-work analysis

access.entityLabel: Untraced Abbey Walk copy reported by David Riddell in 1636

entities.description: CELM records an additional copy of The Abbey Walk reported in David Riddell's 1636 list but not presently traced. It is evidence of a reported witness, not an extant text available for collation.
entities.label: Untraced Abbey Walk copy reported by David Riddell in 1636
notes: Its former holding, text, and relationship to the extant Bannatyne and Maitland versions remain unknown.

next_steps: Ask the Oxford Text Archive or U-M for legacy accession documentation if production-level provenance becomes necessary.
openQuestions.question: Can a surviving OTA deposit record identify the exact production workflow and granularity of U-243-A beyond the 1993 U-M header?

next_steps: Preserve both witness ascriptions and consult future attribution scholarship; do not normalize the conflict into a single certain author.
notes: The selected Bannatyne version ends quod patrik Iohnistoun; the Maitland version ends quod Mr Robert Henrysoun.
openQuestions.question: Who wrote The Thre Deid Pollis?

next_steps: Keep attribution scoped to each poem and transmission layer; review new attribution studies rather than upgrading legacy collection headings.
openQuestions.question: How confidently should The Prais of Aige, The Abbey Walk, and Ane Prayer for the Pest be assigned to Henryson?

next_steps: Retain broad late-fifteenth-century Scottish context unless poem-specific evidence emerges; do not treat the XML's collection-level c. 1480 as exact.
openQuestions.question: What are the individual composition dates and places of the twelve poems?

next_steps: Check Bannatyne directly against a diplomatic transcription and any errata; retain 91 as the inspected text count in the meantime.
openQuestions.question: Why does Smith's source table report 90 lines for Sum Practysis of Medecyne when Smith's printed text and the XML run through line 91?

next_steps: Seek written permission from NLS before publishing or redistributing the cached Gray images; retain the cache for private research validation only.
openQuestions.question: What reuse permission, if any, will NLS grant for the Gray manuscript images marked InC-RUU?

next_steps: Ask NLS rare-books staff or consult the current rare-books catalog before citing a present shelfmark.
openQuestions.question: What is the current precise NLS shelfmark for the mounted Chepman and Myllar print collection formerly designated Advocates Library 19.1.16?

next_steps: Follow future CELM revisions and search Riddell-related catalog records; do not infer readings or a stemmatic position without the copy.
openQuestions.question: Does the Abbey Walk copy reported by David Riddell in 1636 survive under an unidentified shelfmark?

reviewNotes: The cataloging boundary is the twelve DIV1 units actually present in the pinned XML. Smith also prints collateral versions and other shorter poems, including The Want of Wise Men, that are not in HenMino.

Bannatyne draft and main-text passages are parts of one surviving codex, NLS Adv.MS.1.1.6; the codex's current two-volume binding and two digital manifests are facsimile divisions, not separate textual witnesses.

Exact folio, historical page, canvas, and tested image-API links are recorded for every selected manuscript passage. Page images can include text before or after the selected poem at shared boundaries.

For The Thre Deid Pollis, direct inspection places the selected text at current fols. 57v-59r, historical pages 174-177, canvases 192-195. CELM's combined folio/page locator is internally inconsistent because page 178 is the following work.
Gray-manuscript foliation is stated in both current NLS form (fols. 71r-72v) and the historical form used by Smith and CELM (fols. 70r-71v).

Access, the public-domain status of underlying historical texts, licenses or rights statements for modern files, and provider reproduction terms are separate assertions. Public viewing does not by itself establish permission to redistribute images.
The XML's c. 1480 creation date and blanket Henryson heading are legacy collection-level assertions; they are not allowed to erase poem-level date and authorship uncertainty.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a copy or version of University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive electronic item U-243-A, made in 1993 from G. Gregory Smith's 1908 Scottish Text Society edition. The electronic item is an edited selection of twelve separately headed shorter poems, not one poem and not every work that Smith or a modern complete edition groups among Henryson's shorter poems. Smith's selected texts derive from ten passages in the Bannatyne manuscript, the sole Gray-manuscript copy of The Annunciation, and the 1508 Chepman and Myllar print of The Prais of Aige; his apparatus and parallel texts also use Bannatyne alternatives, the Maitland Folio, and the Makculloch manuscript. Authorship is not uniform: several selected witnesses name Henryson, some selected versions are anonymous but have attributed collateral versions, The Prayer for the Pest has a later-hand Henryson ascription, and the selected Bannatyne text of The Thre Deid Pollis names Patrick Johnstoun while the Maitland version names Henryson.

catalogSummary.author: Robert Henryson (traditional and poem-specific; some assignments uncertain or disputed), with Patrick Johnstoun named in the selected witness of The Thre Deid Pollis
catalogSummary.date: Poems traditionally dated to the late fifteenth century; individual composition dates uncertain; edited electronic selection created in 1993
catalogSummary.editor: G. Gregory Smith (1908 source edition); U-M/OTA electronic selection (1993)
catalogSummary.title: The Minor Poems of Robert Henryson (twelve-poem selection)

catalogingSubject.label: The Minor Poems of Robert Henryson (twelve-poem selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual/content unit delimited by the pinned U-M/OTA XML: twelve separately headed Older Scots verse works selected through G. Gregory Smith's 1908 edition. It is not one poem, an authorial collection, every shorter poem printed by Smith, or the complete set of works in the current METS Shorter Poems section. Each top-level poem is modeled as a work part, with the Moralitas of The Bludy Serk as a nested section. Witnesses, print versions, Smith's edition, and the electronic encoding are transmission layers described in the linked lineage manifest.

contentParts.label: Robene and Makyne
notes: The XML has one additional unnumbered L for the subscription after the 128 verse lines.
contentParts.summary: Makyne declares her love to the indifferent shepherd Robene; after she withdraws, he falls in love and pursues her, but she refuses him, reversing their emotional positions.

contentParts.label: The Bludy Serk

locators.value: HenMino:2, narrative lines 1-96; Moralitas at HenMino:2.1

notes: The parent metrics count the twelve narrative stanzas only; the child Moralitas contributes three additional stanzas and 24 lines.
contentParts.summary: A king's daughter is imprisoned by a giant and rescued by a knight who dies of his wounds, leaving his bloody shirt as a token; the nested Moralitas interprets king, lady, giant, knight, pit, and shirt through the Christian story of creation, sin, Christ's sacrifice, hell, and salvation.

contentParts.label: Moralitas

locators.value: 335-336 within the broader Bludy Serk passage

notes: XML L N=121 is the subscription ‘ffinis quod mr r. henrici,’ not a twenty-fifth verse line.
contentParts.summary: The narrative figures are decoded as the Trinity, the human soul, Lucifer, Christ, hell, sin, and the saving memory of Christ's blood, ending with an exhortation to reject sin and remember the bloody shirt.

contentParts.label: The Garmont of Gud Ladeis
contentParts.summary: A virtuous woman's ideal garment is allegorically assembled from moral qualities, including chastity, humility, good conscience, patience, and truth.

contentParts.label: The Prais of Aige

locators.value: tract 7; images 144 lower portion-145 upper portion

notes: Smith's selected print is anonymous; Makculloch and Bannatyne alternatives are collateral, not encoded versions.
contentParts.summary: The speaker praises age for cooling youthful passion, loosening worldly attachment, revealing mutability, and directing thought toward spiritual ends.

contentParts.label: The Ressoning betuix Aige and Zowth
notes: Eight unnumbered L elements are alternating speaker labels, not verse lines. Smith also prints unselected Makculloch, Bannatyne B, and Maitland versions.
contentParts.summary: Youth boasts of pleasure, vitality, and love while Age answers with the brevity of delight, bodily decline, moral danger, and the need to prepare for death.

contentParts.label: The Abbay Walk
notes: The selected draft is anonymous. The Bannatyne main version names Henryson, the Maitland version says authore incerto, and a 1636-reported copy is untraced. One unnumbered XML L is a subscription/display line.
contentParts.summary: A moral lyric urges trust in God and gratitude in both prosperity and adversity, repeating the counsel to accept divine governance rather than worldly instability.

contentParts.label: The Ressoning betuix Deth and Man
notes: Six unnumbered L elements are speaker labels and one is a subscription/display line; none counts toward the 48 verse lines. Canvas 189 is shared with the end of Aige and Zowth.
contentParts.summary: Death and Man dispute the certainty, timing, and spiritual meaning of mortality, pressing the living speaker toward repentance and preparation.

contentParts.label: Aganis Haisty Credence of Titlaris
notes: One unnumbered XML L is a subscription/display line. Smith also prints a collateral Maitland version.
contentParts.summary: A counsel poem warns rulers and great lords not to believe tale-bearers, slanderers, or hurried accusation, urging deliberation, truth, and direct hearing instead.

contentParts.label: The Annunciation
notes: The Gray manuscript is the sole known witness. One unnumbered XML L is a subscription/display line; canvas 178 also begins the following legal entry.
contentParts.summary: A devotional lyric celebrates Gabriel's greeting, Mary's humility and consent, Christ's Incarnation, and the Virgin's intercessory role.

contentParts.label: Sum Practysis of Medecyne
notes: Four unnumbered L elements are recipe subheadings. Smith's source table says 90 lines, but the inspected printed text and XML have an actual final line 91; the discrepancy remains open.
contentParts.summary: A mock medical letter answers bad medicine with grotesque pseudo-recipes, comic Latinized headings, impossible ingredients, bodily humor, and deliberately nonsensical cures.

contentParts.label: The Thre Deid Pollis

locators.scheme: visually corrected folio and historical page

notes: The selected witness ends ‘quod patrik Iohnistoun’; the Maitland version names Robert Henryson. One unnumbered L is the subscription and one is empty. Direct image inspection corrects CELM's internally inconsistent endpoint: page 178/canvas 196 is the next work.
contentParts.summary: Three skulls address the living, especially the young, ornamented women, the proud, and the old, exposing bodily decay and social equality in death and urging penitence and prayer.

contentParts.label: Ane Prayer for the Pest
notes: The XML HEAD is ‘Ane prayer for the pest"’ with a stray trailing quotation mark; the catalog label silently removes that encoding typo. The selected version's Henryson ascription is in a later hand.
contentParts.summary: A communal supplication asks God to turn away plague, forgive sin, reform public and private conduct, protect the people, and restore civic and spiritual health.

dateStatements.display: Traditionally late fifteenth century; individual poem dates are uncertain
notes: The XML's c. 1480 value is a legacy collection-level assertion and is not precise enough to normalize the twelve works individually.
The disputed authorship of The Thre Deid Pollis further cautions against forcing one authorial chronology onto the selection.

scope.description: Broad literary period for the twelve poem texts, not a claim of simultaneous composition or an authorial collection

dateStatements.display: Late fifteenth century, probably c. 1500, in the Gray manuscript
notes: The witness date is not necessarily the date of composition.

dateStatements.display: 1508, in the Chepman and Myllar prints
notes: The printed version is anonymous and is a transmission date, not a composition date.
This dates the electronic selection, not any medieval poem.

scope.description: The exact twelve-poem HenMino selection as a cataloging unit

extent.description: Twelve top-level verse works plus the nested Moralitas in The Bludy Serk. The 106 LG elements contain 867 intellectual verse lines. Raw XML counts are 893 L and 868 numbered L because the encoding also uses L for speaker labels, subheadings, subscriptions, and one empty display line, and numbers the Bludy Serk subscription 121.

assertion.notes: The XML has 893 L elements and 868 numbered L elements, but one numbered L is The Bludy Serk's subscription and 25 unnumbered L elements are non-verse display matter.

formStatements.description: Twelve verse works totaling 867 intellectual verse lines in 106 stanza or verse-group elements; labels, subheadings, subscriptions, and an empty display line are excluded from the verse-line total.

genres.label: Edited selection
notes: This whole-scope primary term describes the heterogeneous cataloging unit rather than forcing one literary genre across all twelve works.

languageStatements.label: Older Scots
notes: Often described more specifically as Middle Scots. Brief Latin subscriptions and formulae do not constitute a sustained secondary-language passage for collection-level faceting.
Content parts preserve the XML's historical spellings where useful, with catalog capitalization normalized; the stray final quotation mark in the Prayer heading is treated as an encoding typo.
Witness dates, foliation, printing places, and digital-publication dates belong to lineage layers unless explicitly scoped here as first attestation or first publication.
No English or Latin language facet is added: the intellectual verse is Older Scots, and brief Latinized headings or subscriptions are embedded formulae rather than sustained passages.
The content-part totals are non-overlapping: The Bludy Serk parent counts twelve narrative stanzas/96 lines and its child Moralitas counts three stanzas/24 lines.

next_steps: Preserve both witness ascriptions and review future attribution scholarship; do not select one author without new evidence.
notes: Bannatyne selected version A names Patrick Johnstoun; Maitland version B names Robert Henryson.
openQuestions.question: Who wrote The Thre Deid Pollis?

next_steps: Keep responsibilities part-scoped and distinguish the selected copy from collateral and later-hand ascriptions; consult new attribution studies before revising confidence.
openQuestions.question: How confidently can The Prais of Aige, The Abbay Walk, and Ane Prayer for the Pest be assigned to Henryson?

next_steps: Use broad late-fifteenth-century Scottish context only; seek poem-specific linguistic, historical, or manuscript evidence before normalizing dates or places.
notes: The XML's c. 1480 value is retained as a legacy assertion, not a secure date for all parts.
openQuestions.question: What are the individual composition dates and places of the twelve poems?

next_steps: Compare the final stanza directly with a diplomatic Bannatyne transcription and any edition errata; retain the inspected 91-line count meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: Why does Smith's source table call Sum Practysis of Medecyne a 90-line poem while the printed text and XML contain 91 verse lines?

next_steps: Seek legacy U-M/OTA project documentation if the selection rationale becomes catalogically important; retain the XML boundary without speculating.
openQuestions.question: Why did the 1993 electronic item select exactly these twelve shorter poems and omit other Smith texts such as The Want of Wise Men?

notes: Edinburgh is the publication place of the selected 1508 print and an association of the Bannatyne codex, not thereby a proven place of composition for all twelve works.
This grouped responsibility is scoped to named parts; it does not authorize a blanket whole-selection attribution.

scope.description: Eight poems whose selected source copy or immediate witness tradition explicitly names Henryson and whose attribution is accepted in current scholarship

assertion.notes: Smith's selected Chepman and Myllar print version is anonymous; a collateral Bannatyne version attributes the poem to Henryson.

notes: Authorship and selected-copy attribution are deliberately not conflated.

assertion.notes: The selected Bannatyne draft is anonymous; the Bannatyne main version names Henryson, while the Maitland version says authore incerto.

notes: The selected draft copy's silence is retained as transmission evidence.

assertion.notes: The selected Bannatyne main version's Henryson ascription is in a later hand.

notes: The later-hand subscription supports an attribution history, not certainty of authorship.

assertion.notes: The collateral Maitland text names Henryson, but the selected Bannatyne text names Patrick Johnstoun.

notes: This records a competing witness attribution, not a resolved author assignment.

assertion.notes: Confirmed as the explicit subscription in the selected Bannatyne witness, not confirmed as the true intellectual authorship.

notes: Smith prints Finis: quod patrik Iohnistoun at the end of selected version A.
Editorial responsibility belongs to a transmission layer but is included because this cataloging unit is an edited selection whose text and boundaries depend on Smith.

scope.description: The 1908 source-edition presentation and source-version selection, not authorship of the poems

notes: Smith's preface thanks Stevenson for transcribing and collating texts.

scope.description: Transcription work for Smith's source edition
Poems for which Smith prints or compares multiple source versions

subjects.label: Love, courtship, and reversal of desire
The Annunciation, Incarnation, and Marian devotion

summaries.value: This edited selection gathers twelve varied Older Scots poems transmitted under or near Robert Henryson's name: a pastoral courtship dialogue, a Christian rescue allegory with moralitas, an allegorical garment of virtues, praises and debates concerning age, moral counsels on fortune and slander, a dialogue between Death and Man, an Annunciation lyric, a burlesque medical recipe, a three-skull memento mori, and a plague prayer. Their shared concerns include mutability, mortality, ethical self-government, salvation, and the instability of worldly desire, but the selection is editorial rather than an authorial book and its attributions are not uniform.

The record stops at the twelve DIV1 units in HenMino.xml. It includes the nested Moralitas of The Bludy Serk, but excludes Smith's parallel source versions and shorter works absent from the XML, including The Want of Wise Men. Source subscriptions and collateral ascriptions are retained at poem and witness level rather than flattened into the electronic header's blanket Henryson attribution.

The pinned XML contains 893 L elements: 867 intellectual verse lines, one wrongly numbered subscription in The Bludy Serk, and 25 unnumbered labels, subscriptions, medicine subheads, or an empty display line. Page milestones reflect Smith's printed pages but are incomplete and anomalous. Content-part metrics therefore use inspected verse lines and groups, not raw element counts.

notes: The qualifier makes the electronic item's exact boundary visible and avoids implying that this is a complete or authorial collection.
titles.value: The Minor Poems of Robert Henryson (twelve-poem selection)

notes: Exact electronic-item title; its blanket possessive authorship is a discovery label, not a resolution of poem-level attribution conflicts.

Their shared concerns include mutability, mortality, ethical self-government, salvation, and the instability of worldly desire, but the selection is editorial rather than an authorial book and its attributions are not uniform.
